titleStmt.title: Letter from Joseph Slevin to the Longford Urban District Council, 8 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Joseph Slevin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Joseph Slevin to the Longford Urban District Council on 8 August 1916. In the letter Joseph Slevin submits to the Longford Urban District Council his report as Town Surveyor. Joseph Slevin (b. 1856) was the Town Surveyor for Longford. The Longford Urban District Council was the local authority and was renamed the Longford Town Council in 2002.

correspAction.placeName: Longford Urban District Council, Longford, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Town Surveyor & Water Inspectors Report Mr Chairman & Gentlemen I beg to report
that Mr Dergin made an inspection on Friday
last of both Resevoirs and walked the supply
stream from Goshen to the well house. I
pointed out to him that there was no provision
made for the drainage of the labourers cottage
recently built which he made a note
of — J.
I find it impossible to get a person to empty the
pits at the back of the corporation houses.
As this is a matter of pressing importance in
the interest of the occupiers of the houses
adjoining and in fact the health of the Town
some means must be devised to have the
nuisance abated at once. There are complaints
about the sanitary condition of Bog Lane. Both Mr Healy and the late Mr Farrell
agreed to have a gate put up at each end
but for some reason the matter was let
drag on. I think it would be well if there
was an order made tonight to have the work
carried out at once. I was informed that there were 2 lambs
that died from arsenical poisoning in a
store in a yard off Main Street. I immediately
went to the place and found the lambs in an
advanced state of decomposition. I got the
owner to sprinkle them with carbolic and
lime and saw them buried the following
morning. I propose getting concrete footpath in
Killashee Street commenced this week. Your obedient Servant Joseph Slevin 8th August 1916
